UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile within the application, distinct from authentication credentials. It stores user-specific data relevant to the application's functionality.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

email.description: The user's email address, primarily for display and communication purposes, not for direct authentication.

firstName.description: The first name of the user.

lastName.description: The last name of the user.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was initially created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating the last time the user profile was updated.

Task.title: Task
Task.description: Represents a single task created and managed by a user within the application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Task entity.

userProfileId.description: Reference to the UserProfile that owns this task. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Task)

title.description: The main title or brief summary of the task.

description.description: A detailed description providing more context or specifics about the task.

dueDate.description: The specific date and time by which the task is expected to be completed.

priority.description: The priority level assigned to the task, e.g., 'Low', 'Medium', 'High', 'Urgent'.

status.description: The current progress status of the task, e.g., 'To Do', 'In Progress', 'Done', 'Blocked'.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the task was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating the last time the task was updated.

SubTask.title: SubTask
SubTask.description: Represents a smaller, distinct action item that contributes to the completion of a larger parent task.

id.description: Unique identifier for the SubTask entity.

taskId.description: Reference to the parent Task this sub-task belongs to. (Relationship: Task 1:N SubTask)

title.description: The title or brief description of the sub-task.

isCompleted.description: A boolean flag indicating whether the sub-task has been completed or not.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the sub-task was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating the last time the sub-task was updated.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles. The document ID `{userId}` matches the Firebase Authentication UID (`request.auth.uid`), ensuring direct ownership. This collection represents private data owned directly by the user.

Contains tasks created by a specific user. Each task document includes the `userProfileId` field, which must match `{userId}` in the path and `request.auth.uid`. This denormalization ensures authorization independence and simplifies security rules, making it a private collection for the owning user.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns the task. Matches `request.auth.uid`.

params.name: taskId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific task.

definition.description: Stores sub-tasks associated with a specific task. Each sub-task document includes denormalized `userProfileId` (from the parent task's owner) and `taskId` to ensure authorization independence. This allows rules to verify ownership without fetching parent documents. This is a private collection nested under a user's task.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns the parent task. Matches `request.auth.uid`.

params.name: taskId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent task this sub-task belongs to.

params.name: subTaskId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific sub-task.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure for TaskFlow Pro is designed around the principle of user-centric data ownership and authorization independence. All user-specific data, including profiles, tasks, and sub-tasks, are organized hierarchically under a dedicated user document. This structure directly maps to Firebase Authentication UIDs, simplifying security rules and ensuring data privacy.

**Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):**
This design achieves authorization independence by leveraging path-based ownership and denormalization. For UserProfile, the document ID directly corresponds to `request.auth.uid`, making authorization trivial (`allow read, write: if request.auth.uid == userId;`).

For Tasks, they are stored in a subcollection `/users/{userId}/tasks/{taskId}`. The `Task` document itself contains a `userProfileId` field, which effectively denormalizes the owner's ID. This allows security rules to authorize access by checking `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userProfileId` without requiring any `get()` operations to the parent `/users/{userId}` document. This is crucial for atomic operations and robust rule evaluation.

Similarly, for SubTasks, stored at `/users/{userId}/tasks/{taskId}/subTasks/{subTaskId}`, the `userProfileId` (owner of the parent Task) and `taskId` (ID of the parent Task) are denormalized into each `SubTask` document. This means a SubTask document carries all necessary authorization context (who owns it, which task it belongs to) within itself. Rules can simply check `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userProfileId` to ensure the sub-task is being accessed by its legitimate owner, again avoiding costly and complex `get()` calls.

**QAPs (Queries as Permissions):**
The structural segregation ensures that `list` operations are inherently secure and efficient. Because all tasks and sub-tasks are nested under `/users/{userId}/...`, any `list` query issued by a client will naturally be scoped to the authenticated user's data (e.g., querying `/users/my_uid/tasks`). Security rules will then enforce that `userId` in the path must match `request.auth.uid`, thereby automatically satisfying QAPs. This prevents users from inadvertently or maliciously listing data belonging to other users. There is no mix of public and private data in the same collections, ensuring a homogeneous security posture for all documents within a given collection path. This eliminates the need for complex, performance-impacting filtering logic within security rules for `list` operations, making them fast, predictable, and secure.
